children.name: Bubo
children.description: Eagle-owls and the Snowy Owl — the largest and most powerful owls on Earth. The Eurasian Eagle-owl is the apex nocturnal predator of the Palearctic, taking prey up to the size of roe deer fawns and young foxes. Its territorial call — a deep, resonant 'ooh-hu' — carries several kilometres on still nights. The Snowy Owl is diurnal, active in the perpetual Arctic summer daylight, its white plumage providing camouflage against snow. Eagle-owls were eliminated from much of Europe in the 19th century and are now recovering through reintroduction.

children.name: Bubo bubo
children.description: The largest owl in the world. Wingspan up to 188 cm. Orange eyes, prominent ear tufts, and a booming call give it a formidable presence. Now recolonising parts of Scandinavia and central Europe after centuries of persecution.

children.name: Bubo scandiacus
children.description: Breeding success is tightly linked to lemming cycles; in irruption years young owls disperse far south. Males become nearly pure white with age; females retain dark barring throughout their lives. Famously associated with Harry Potter's Hedwig.

children.name: Bubo virginianus
children.description: The most widespread and adaptable owl in the Americas, from tundra to tropical forest. The first owl to nest in the year, often incubating eggs through January snow. Its grip is reported to require around 30 kg of force to open.

children.name: Bubo africanus
children.description: The most common large owl across sub-Saharan Africa, frequently encountered in parks, gardens, and rocky hillsides. Its haunting double hoot is a signature sound of the African night. Pale grey-brown with heavy dark spotting, yellow eyes, and prominent ear tufts.

children.name: Bubo lacteus
children.description: The largest owl in Africa, named after the French naturalist Jules Verreaux. Its distinctive pink eyelids are unique among owls. It regularly preys on large mammals including monkeys and hedgehogs.

children.name: Bubo capensis
children.description: A powerful eagle-owl of rocky koppies and mountainous terrain across eastern and southern Africa. Prey includes francolins, hares, and mongooses. The three subspecies differ in size and the intensity of the brown streaking on the underparts.

children.name: Bubo nipalensis
children.description: A large, forest eagle-owl of the Himalayas and Southeast Asian hill forests, named for the bold heart-shaped spots on its pale underparts. Its call — a prolonged, eerie wail — is considered an omen of death in parts of its range. It hunts large birds, civets, and hares.

children.name: Bubo coromandus
children.description: A dark-plumaged eagle-owl of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, favouring riparian woodland and well-wooded areas near rivers and lakes. The call is a series of deep, booming hoots. It is closely associated with old mango and banyan trees in South Asian villages.

children.name: Bubo shelleyi
children.description: One of the least known large owls in the world, confined to the primary rainforest of West and Central Africa. Named after the British ornithologist George Ernest Shelley. Fewer than a handful of specimens have ever been collected and it is almost never recorded in the wild.

children.name: Bubo sumatranus
children.description: A medium-large eagle-owl of the Sundaic lowland rainforests, distinguished by its finely barred underparts and prominent horizontal ear tufts. It occupies primary and tall secondary forest from the Malay Peninsula to Borneo. Rapid deforestation across its range is causing serious population declines.

children.name: Bubo poensis
children.description: A medium-sized eagle owl from Central African rainforests; named after the collector.

children.name: Bubo vosseleri
children.description: A rare endemic to the Usambara Mountains of Tanzania; threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Bubo philippensis
children.description: A large eagle owl endemic to the Philippines; dusky brown with prominent ear tufts.

children.name: Bubo poensis
children.description: A medium-sized African eagle-owl; rich brown with dark streaks; strictly nocturnal.

children.name: Bubo lacteus
children.description: The largest African owl; pale grey with pink eyelids; powerful predator of large prey.

children.name: Bubo nisus
children.description: A large owl of Southeast Asian forests; heavily spotted belly and prominent ear tufts.

children.name: Bubo sumatranus
children.description: A striped eagle-owl with fine barring on the underparts; named after Sumatra.

children.name: Strix
children.description: Wood owls — large to medium-sized owls with round heads (no ear tufts) and dark, liquid eyes. The Tawny Owl is the most familiar owl of European woodland, its quavering 'hoo-oo-oo' and sharp 'ke-wick' making it the classic sound of a woodland night — though the 'tu-whit tu-whoo' of English folklore is a duet between the two calls given by different individuals. Wood owls are highly territorial and sedentary; a Tawny Owl born in a wood may spend its entire life within a square kilometre.

children.name: Strix aluco
children.description: The most numerous owl in Europe. Almost entirely nocturnal; facial disc acts as a parabolic reflector to concentrate sound to asymmetrically positioned ears — it can strike prey in complete darkness by sound alone.

children.name: Strix uralensis
children.description: A boreal forest owl that has expanded westward from Scandinavia into central Europe in recent decades. Known for fierce defence of nest sites — it will strike humans on the head who approach too close.

children.name: Strix nebulosa
children.description: The largest owl by length but lighter than the Eagle-owl; much of its bulk is feather. It can hear voles moving under 60 cm of snow and plunge through the surface to capture them. Its enormous facial disc functions as a giant ear trumpet.

children.name: Strix varia
children.description: Strix varia (Barred Owl) is a wood owls species found in North America region.

children.name: Strix occidentalis
children.description: A flagship species of old-growth temperate rainforest in western North America, now critically threatened by competition from the invasive Barred Owl. Requires large, ancient trees with cavities and complex structure. The Northern Spotted Owl subspecies has become a symbol of the old-growth logging controversy.

children.name: Strix hylophila
children.description: A medium-sized wood owl restricted to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina and Paraguay. Rusty-orange barring on the underparts distinguishes it from related species. Like the forest it depends on, it is threatened by ongoing fragmentation and loss of habitat.

children.name: Strix nigrolineata
children.description: A striking owl of tropical lowland forest from Mexico to Ecuador, boldly patterned in black and white bars. It is often attracted to artificial lights where it hunts large insects, bats, and small birds. A forest interior species that is sensitive to deforestation across its Central and South American range.

children.name: Strix woodfordii
children.description: The most widespread wood owl in sub-Saharan Africa, inhabiting forest, woodland, and well-wooded gardens. Its duetting pairs produce a bubbly, hooting call. Named after the British naval officer and ornithologist Smith Woodford.

children.name: Strix seloputo
children.description: A large, orange-faced wood owl of lowland forest and plantation in Southeast Asia, from Myanmar to the Philippines. It has adapted well to parks and large gardens in Singapore and Malaysia, where it nests in old rain trees. The Javan subspecies is now sometimes treated as a separate species.

children.name: Strix fulvescens
children.description: A mesoamerican highland species; closely related to S. varia but with richer coloration.

children.name: Strix chacoensis
children.description: A little-known owl of the dry Chaco forests of Paraguay and Argentina.

children.name: Strix rufipes
children.description: A South American wood owl with red legs; found in temperate forests of Chile and Argentina.

children.name: Strix leptogrammica
children.description: A medium-sized Asian owl; finely barred underparts and dark brown back.

children.name: Strix nivicola
children.description: A high-altitude Strix from Himalayan forests; adapted to cold montane habitats.

children.name: Strix occidentalis
children.description: A controversial endangered species of old-growth forests; brown with white spots.

children.name: Strix nebulosa
children.description: The largest Strix by length; massive facial disc; hunts voles in boreal forests.

children.name: Asio
children.description: Eared owls — medium-sized owls with erectile ear tufts used in communication and camouflage (they bear no relation to the actual ears). The Long-eared Owl is a secretive species of conifer plantations and hedgerows; its roost sites — communal in winter, with a characteristic drop of elongated pellets and whitewash below the roost tree — are often the easiest way to find one. The Short-eared Owl is a diurnal hunter of open moorland and grassland, quartering low and buoyant like a harrier.

children.name: Asio otus
children.description: A cryptic species that presses flat against tree bark to disappear. Ear tufts are raised in alarm, giving it a slender, branch-like profile. Winter roosts in dense ivy or hawthorn may hold dozens of birds.

children.name: Asio flammeus
children.description: One of the world's most widely distributed owls — found on every continent except Australia and Antarctica. A ground-nester of moorland, marsh, and tundra. Numbers fluctuate with vole populations.

children.name: Asio stygius
children.description: A dark, diabolical-looking owl of highland forest from Mexico to northern Argentina, earning its name from the mythological Styx. Its blackish-brown plumage and flame-orange eyes give it a particularly intense expression. Rarely observed despite being widespread; its habits remain poorly known.

children.name: Asio clamator
children.description: A pale, heavily streaked owl of open grassland, marshes, and savanna from Mexico to Argentina. Unlike most Asio owls, it often hunts during the day. The loud, barking call gives it its scientific name 'clamator' (the one who cries out).

children.name: Asio abyssinicus
children.description: An African highland species from Ethiopia and eastern Africa; similar to the long-eared owl.

children.name: Asio helenae
children.description: A newly described species from the Solomons; named after the naturalist's wife.

children.name: Asio abyssinicus
children.description: A highland owl from the Ethiopian Plateau; greyish-brown with long ear tufts.

children.name: Asio stridus
children.description: A striking Neotropical owl; heavily striped underparts and dark facial disc.

children.name: Asio capensis
children.description: A medium-sized owl of African wetlands; short ear tufts and pale face.

children.name: Athene
children.description: The Little Owl — introduced to Britain in the 19th century (natives of southern and central Europe), now well established in lowland farmland and parkland. Despite being named for the Greek goddess of wisdom, the Little Owl is, like all owls, not particularly intelligent relative to other birds. It is however diurnal and crepuscular, often sitting conspicuously on fence posts, telegraph poles, and old walls in full daylight. It bobs its head repeatedly when agitated, lending it an almost comical character.

children.name: Athene noctua
children.description: Introduced to England by Lord Lilford in the 1870s–1880s. Now a widespread farmland species. Nests in tree holes, cliff crevices, and old buildings. Eats earthworms, beetles, and small rodents.

children.name: Athene brama
children.description: Perhaps the most familiar owl across the Indian subcontinent, abundant in villages, towns, and agricultural land from Pakistan to Indochina. It often roosts in pairs in holes in buildings and trees, and is tolerant of human presence. A noisy owl with a repertoire of churring, screeching calls.

children.name: Athene cunicularia
children.description: Uniquely among Strigidae, this small owl nests underground, using abandoned burrows of prairie dogs, ground squirrels, and armadillos — or digging its own. It is active during the day and bobs its head comically. The Florida population is threatened by urban development.

children.name: Athene bramus
children.description: A small greyish owlet of South Asia; white-spotted head and underparts.

children.name: Athene superciliaris
children.description: A tiny owl from Timor and nearby islands; prominent white brow.

children.name: Athene nightgem
children.description: A recently described owlet from the Philippines; iridescent feather sheen.

children.name: Glaucidium
children.description: Pygmy owls — the smallest owls in their respective regions and, gram for gram, some of the most formidable predators in the avian world. The Eurasian Pygmy Owl routinely kills prey — small birds and voles — equal to its own bodyweight. It has false 'eyes' (ocelli) on the back of its head, visible when it turns away from a mobbing bird, which may serve to deter attack or divert the mobber's focus. Pygmy owls cache prey for consumption during winter shortages.

children.name: Glaucidium passerinum
children.description: Europe's smallest owl at around 60–80 g. Despite its size, it can kill small thrushes. Breeding in old Black Woodpecker holes, it is a beneficiary of old-growth boreal forest. Song is a simple, ascending series of whistles repeated endlessly.

children.name: Glaucidium gnoma
children.description: A tiny predator of montane conifer forests in western North America, feared by songbirds far out of proportion to its 65 g weight. It regularly hunts prey larger than itself including small mammals and passerines. Often active at dawn and dusk, its tooting call can provoke mobbing from chickadees and nuthatches.

children.name: Glaucidium brasilianum
children.description: A widespread and common pygmy owl of open woodland, scrub, and forest edge from Arizona and Texas south to Argentina. Rusty-brown above with a streaked breast; active during the day. One of the most frequently encountered owls across tropical America.

children.name: Glaucidium perlatum
children.description: The most conspicuous small owl of African woodland savanna, frequently seen and heard during the day. Its rising, whistling call is one of the characteristic sounds of the African bush. Like other Glaucidium, it has a striking false-face pattern on the back of its head.

children.name: Glaucidium capense
children.description: A barred pygmy owl of forest and dense woodland in eastern and central Africa, somewhat larger than the Pearl-spotted Owlet. Its call is a sustained series of rapid, purring hoots. Often found in miombo woodland and riparian forest.

children.name: Glaucidium cuculoides
children.description: One of the largest pygmy owls, widespread across foothills and montane forest from Nepal to China and Southeast Asia. It is frequently heard at dusk delivering a loud, accelerating series of hoots. Despite its small size, it is a bold predator of lizards, small mammals, and large insects.

children.name: Glaucidium costatum
children.description: A small pygmy owl from the northern Andes; barred tail and streaked underparts.

children.name: Glaucidium nanum
children.description: The southernmost Glaucidium; found in Chile and Argentina; fearless predator of birds larger than itself.

children.name: Glaucidium palmatum
children.description: Endemic to Costa Rica's highlands; named for the palm-like pattern on its crown.

children.name: Otus
children.description: Scops owls — a large and diverse genus of mostly small, cryptically patterned owls of warm climates across the Old World and tropics. The Eurasian Scops Owl is a summer visitor to southern Europe, returning from Africa to fill the warm Mediterranean night with its repetitive, monotonous, piercing whistle — 'tyuup...tyuup...tyuup' — for hours from March onwards. Its cryptic bark-pattern plumage and habit of pressing itself upright against a branch makes it almost invisible in daylight.

children.name: Otus scops
children.description: The archetypal sound of the southern European summer night. A long-distance migrant wintering in sub-Saharan Africa; populations are declining due to insecticide use reducing prey availability in both breeding and wintering areas.

children.name: Otus bakkamoena
children.description: A widespread scops owl of the Indian subcontinent and Sri Lanka, inhabiting open woodland, forest edges, and well-wooded gardens. Its monotonous single-note call 'wut' is repeated persistently throughout the night. Plumage varies between brown and grey morphs.

children.name: Otus sunia
children.description: A migratory scops owl breeding across East Asia from India to Japan and wintering in Southeast Asia. One of the most common small owls in Japanese and Chinese forests. Separated from the Indian Scops Owl by molecular studies; the two are virtually identical in the field.

children.name: Otus elegans
children.description: An island scops owl distributed across the Ryukyu Islands of Japan and adjacent island groups. Several subspecies on small or remote islands may warrant full species status. A forest owl threatened by deforestation and invasive species, particularly on the Ryukyu and Batanes islands.

children.name: Otus senegalensis
children.description: A tiny, cryptically patterned scops owl of sub-Saharan Africa, remarkably difficult to detect by sight as it roosts pressed upright against bark. Its monotonous, frog-like 'pruup' is one of the ubiquitous sounds of the African night. It relies heavily on insects but also takes small lizards.

children.name: Otus flammeolus
children.description: A tiny, dark-eyed scops owl (unusual in the family — most have yellow eyes) of high-elevation pine forests in western North America. Nearly entirely insectivorous, migrating to Central America in winter. Despite being locally common, it is rarely seen owing to its cryptic habits.

children.name: Otus leucotis
children.description: Known for its extraordinary threat-display in which it flattens its plumage and extends its wings to reveal a broad cape of patterned feathers — mimicking a much larger bird. This species achieved viral fame through a video of the display going around the internet. Found in semi-arid woodland and savanna across the Sahel zone.

children.name: Otus rutilus
children.description: A scops owl restricted to the humid forests of Madagascar and the Comoros. Its rapid trilling call distinguishes it from the similar Madagascar Scops Owl. The species demonstrates the high endemism of Madagascar's avifauna, with several closely related forms on different islands.

children.name: Otus magnus
children.description: The largest Otus species; found in the Philippines; distinctive deep resonant hoot.

children.name: Otus booki
children.description: A recently described scops owl from the Moluccas; named after the collector.

children.name: Otus bakkamoena
children.description: A small greyish owl of South Asian woodlands; distinctive black-rimmed facial disc.

children.name: Otus magnus
children.description: A recently described large scops owl from the Philippines.

children.name: Otus mantananensis
children.description: A small scops owl restricted to islands off Borneo; pale rufous morph.

children.name: Ninox
children.description: Hawk-owls — a diverse genus of medium to large owls distributed across Asia, Australasia, and the Pacific, characterised by long tails, hawk-like facial structure (with a reduced facial disc), and strong, direct flight. Many species are endemic to individual islands or island groups. The Powerful Owl of Australia is one of the world's largest owls; the various Ninox of Pacific islands include some of the most range-restricted owls on Earth.

children.name: Ninox novaeseelandiae
children.description: New Zealand's only surviving native owl (the Laughing Owl went extinct in the early 20th century). Its name is onomatopoeic — the 'more-pork' call rings out from forests throughout both main islands. An adaptable predator of insects, small birds, and rodents that has survived introduction of cats and rats.

children.name: Ninox strenua
children.description: Australia's largest owl and one of the world's most powerful avian predators relative to size. Hunts possums and gliders — often roosting by day holding a large possum carcass in its talons. Old-growth forest is essential for nesting and is seriously threatened by logging.

children.name: Ninox connivens
children.description: Named for its double-note barking call, which can be startlingly loud and dog-like. A widespread owl of open woodland and scrub across mainland Australia and New Guinea. Its blood-curdling 'screaming woman' call variant is said to be the origin of some bushranger legends in colonial Australia.

children.name: Ninox scutulata
children.description: A widely distributed hawk-owl across South and Southeast Asia, conspicuous for its cuckoo-like double hoot delivered repeatedly from forest edges at dusk. It habitually hunts large insects, small birds, and lizards in a swift, direct flight. Migratory in the northern parts of its range.

children.name: Ninox boobook
children.description: Australia's most common and widespread owl, found in virtually every habitat type from desert to rainforest. Its persistent 'boo-book' two-note hoot is familiar throughout the continent. Recent splits have separated several former subspecies (including the Norfolk Island population) as distinct species.

children.name: Ninox philippensis
children.description: A widespread but variable hawk-owl endemic to the Philippine archipelago. Multiple island populations are morphologically and vocally distinct and several have been elevated to full species. A forest interior bird threatened by the extensive deforestation of lowland Philippines.

children.name: Ninox rudis
children.description: A New Guinean hawk owl with rough textured tarsi; poorly known.

children.name: Ninox terror
children.description: A recently discovered hawk owl from the Solomon Islands; named for its aggressive defence of nests.

children.name: Megascops
children.description: Screech-owls — a New World group of small, eared owls largely replacing Otus in the Americas, separated into their own genus based on molecular evidence. Most species are cryptically patterned in grey or rufous morphs matching the bark of trees they roost against. Screech-owls are nocturnal insectivores and small vertebrate predators of forest, woodland, and scrub habitats. Several species have tiny island or mountain ranges and are of conservation concern.

children.name: Megascops asio
children.description: The most familiar small owl of eastern North America, inhabiting wooded suburbs, parks, and orchards as readily as natural forest. Comes in grey and rufous colour morphs. Its quavering whinny and long, descending tremolo are classic sounds of the eastern night.

children.name: Megascops kennicottii
children.description: The western counterpart to the Eastern Screech-owl, inhabiting woodland, desert scrub with large cacti, and riparian trees from Alaska to Mexico. Vocally distinct — its call is a series of short whistles accelerating to a trill. Named after the American naturalist Robert Kennicott.

children.name: Megascops trichopsis
children.description: A small screech-owl of pine-oak woodland in the mountains of southern Arizona and Mexico southward to Nicaragua. Named for its bristly facial whiskers. Its staccato call — a series of evenly pitched hoots — distinguishes it from the overlapping Western Screech-owl.

children.name: Megascops choliba
children.description: The most widespread screech-owl in South America, ranging from Costa Rica to Argentina in open woodland, savanna, and urban parks. Highly adaptable to disturbed habitats. The call is a toad-like trill that is a common night sound across the Neotropics.

children.name: Megascops guatemalae
children.description: A complex of screech-owls of Middle America, recently revised based on vocal differences; several populations formerly included here are now treated as separate species. Inhabits humid lowland and foothill forest from Mexico to Panama. Identification relies heavily on voice.

children.name: Megascops barbarus
children.description: A small screech owl from the highlands of Guatemala and Chiapas; restricted range.

children.name: Megascops cooperi
children.description: A Central American species; Pacific slope distribution; distinctive whistled duet.

children.name: Megascops petersoni
children.description: A Peruvian and Ecuadorian screech owl; rich cinnamon colour; named after Roger Tory Peterson.

children.name: Pulsatrix
children.description: Spectacled owls — robust, striking owls of Neotropical forest with bold facial patterns of dark and white markings creating the impression of spectacles. They are large-headed, powerful hunters of humid lowland rainforest across Central and South America. Young birds are strikingly white with dark facial markings — the inverse of the adults. The genus is named from the Latin 'pulsatrix' (striker), reflecting their forceful hunting style.

children.name: Pulsatrix perspicillata
children.description: The largest owl in Central America, a powerful predator of Neotropical rainforest from Mexico to Bolivia. The white 'spectacles' and yellow eyes on a dark chocolate-brown head make it unmistakable. It hunts possums, bats, large insects, and birds.

children.name: Pulsatrix koeniswaldiana
children.description: A medium-sized spectacled owl restricted to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Paraguay and Argentina. It differs from the Spectacled Owl in its warm tawny-buff brow and smaller size. Named after the German palaeontologist Gustav Heinrich Ralph von Koenigswald.

children.name: Pulsatrix melanota
children.description: A darkly plumaged spectacled owl of cloud forest along the Andes foothills, from Ecuador south to Bolivia. Distinguished from the Spectacled Owl by a dark belly band. Poorly known; found in humid montane forest between 500 and 1,800 m.

children.name: Pulsatrix ortrudis
children.description: A lowland Amazonian species; recently split from P. melanota; dark chocolate-brown with white spectacles.

children.name: Surnia
children.description: The Northern Hawk-owl — a diurnal, hawk-like owl of the boreal forest that perches conspicuously at the top of dead trees scanning for voles, and hunts in full daylight with swift, direct, Accipiter-like flight. It is an irruptive species that appears irregularly south of its normal range in winters when prey populations crash. Unusual among owls in that it relies more on vision than hearing, lacking the strongly asymmetric ear placement of nocturnal hunters.

children.name: Surnia ulula
children.description: Named for its falcon-like silhouette and hunting behaviour. Long tail and narrow wings produce a darting, accipiter-like flight. In vole-crash years, individuals wander far south and are remarkably approachable.

children.name: Surnia ulula
children.description: A diurnal owl of boreal forests; long pointed wings and swift hawk-like flight.

children.name: Aegolius
children.description: Small, large-headed owls with exceptionally developed hearing — the Boreal Owl and Saw-whet Owl are among the most acoustically sensitive birds known, capable of detecting prey under deep snow. The asymmetrically placed ear openings are covered by a broad, flat facial disc. These are owls of dense conifer and boreal forest, largely overlooked owing to their small size and secretive, nocturnal habits. Aegolius comes from the Greek for 'screech-owl'.

children.name: Aegolius funereus
children.description: A small owl of dense boreal and subalpine conifer forest across the Holarctic, also known as Tengmalm's Owl in Europe. Males call persistently in late winter to attract females to nest cavities, often old Black Woodpecker holes. Highly nomadic in response to vole population cycles.

children.name: Aegolius acadicus
children.description: North America's smallest owl east of the Rockies, weighing only 75–100 g. Despite its diminutive size, it is a fierce predator specialising in deer mice and other small rodents. The 'saw-whet' call sounds like a saw being whetted on a stone. Autumn migration through forest corridors is extensive.

children.name: Aegolius ridgwayi
children.description: A little-known small owl of cloud forest in southern Mexico and Central America (Guatemala, Honduras, Costa Rica, Panama), named after the American ornithologist Robert Ridgway. Unlike its relatives, the crown is unspotted. Restricted to mature highland forest above 2,000 m, where it is rarely observed.

children.name: Aegolius harrisii
children.description: A small owl from South American montane and lowland forests; named after Edward Harris.

children.name: Aegolius acadicus
children.description: A tiny North American owl; reddish-brown with a streaked white forehead.

children.name: Aegolius ridgwayi
children.description: A highland owl from Costa Rica to Bolivia; unique among Aegolius for lacking spots.

children.name: Micrathene
children.description: The Elf Owl — the world's smallest and lightest owl, weighing around 40 g. Confined to arid and semi-arid desert scrub and canyon woodland in the American Southwest and Mexico, it is perhaps the only owl that is nearly entirely insectivorous throughout the year. It nests in old woodpecker holes in saguaro cacti and sycamores. Migratory, wintering in Mexico. Despite its tiny size, it is fierce and will defend its nest vigorously.

children.name: Micrathene whitneyi
children.description: The world's lightest owl at 40 g — lighter than a sparrow. Nests in saguaro cactus holes excavated by Gila Woodpeckers and Gilded Flickers. Almost entirely insectivorous; catches moths, scorpions (removing the stinger), and crickets. Named after the American geologist Josiah Dwight Whitney.

children.name: Lophostrix
children.description: The Crested Owl — a distinctive medium-large owl of Neotropical forest with conspicuous long white ear tufts against a dark brown plumage. Its large yellow eyes and pale facial disc give it a distinctive 'surprised' expression. A poorly known species of lowland and foothill humid forest. The genus is monotypic and has no close relatives; its taxonomic position within Strigidae has been debated.

children.name: Lophostrix cristata
children.description: A distinctive owl of humid tropical forest from southern Mexico to Bolivia and Amazonian Brazil. It roosts by day in the understorey, relying on its cryptic plumage to avoid detection. The long white ear tufts, unique in Neotropical owls, are readily visible when the bird is alert.

children.name: Xenoglaux
children.description: The Long-whiskered Owlet — one of the world's most enigmatic owls, discovered in Peru in 1976 and known from only a handful of specimens and observations. It is tiny (about 13 cm), with striking long rictal bristles (whiskers) on its face. It was not photographed alive in the wild until 2007. Confined to elfin cloud forest above 1,800 m on the east Andean slope in northern Peru, where it is threatened by forest clearance.

children.name: Xenoglaux loweryi
children.description: One of the rarest and least known owls in the world, described to science only in 1977 from specimens collected in the Peruvian Andes. The extraordinarily long facial bristles are unique among owls. Named in honour of the American ornithologist George H. Lowery Jr.

children.name: Jubula
children.description: The Maned Owl — a monotypic genus from the lowland rainforests of Central Africa. It has a shaggy, tousled crown and long, loose ear tufts that give it a distinctly maned appearance. A poorly known forest owl, rarely observed and little studied. Formerly placed within Lophostrix; molecular work revealed it is not closely related to the Crested Owl, confirming its placement in a separate genus.

children.name: Jubula lettii
children.description: A large-eyed, shaggy-crested owl of primary lowland rainforest in West and Central Africa, from Liberia to the Democratic Republic of Congo. Seldom encountered and poorly known, with its biology and vocalisation remaining largely undescribed. Named after the Belgian naturalist Marcel Lett.

children.name: Nesasio
children.description: The Fearful Owl — a large, powerful owl endemic to the Solomon Islands, where it is the apex avian predator. Its local name and scientific genus name both reference the fear it inspires. It is a poorly known island endemic that hunts large prey including flying foxes and arboreal marsupials. Classified as Vulnerable owing to ongoing deforestation of the Solomon Islands lowland forests.

children.name: Nesasio solomonensis
children.description: A large, rufous-brown owl endemic to the Solomon Islands, one of the most poorly known owls in the Pacific. Its massive talons and powerful build suggest it takes prey as large as flying foxes. Threatened by deforestation; the largest remaining populations are on Bougainville and Guadalcanal.

children.name: Pseudoscops
children.description: A monotypic genus of medium-sized owl endemic to Jamaica; distinctive heart-shaped facial disc.

children.name: Pseudoscops grammicus
children.description: Endemic to Jamaica; cryptic brown plumage; feeds on insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Rhinoptynx
children.description: A slender, long-winged owl of open habitats in Mexico and South America.

children.name: Rhinoptynx clamator
children.description: A pale, heavily striped owl of savannas and marsh edges; long ear tufts point horizontally.

children.name: Scops
children.description: A group of small scops owls associated with riverine habitats in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Psilops
children.description: Large powerful owl-like birds of the New Guinea highlands; convergent with Buteo in shape.

children.name: Psilops altus
children.description: A large owl of New Guinea highland forests; brown with barred underparts.
